text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Medical Oncology — Gastrointestinal Oncology Program

**CHIEF COMPLAINT**
Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old woman with borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma (ypT2N0M0 after neoadjuvant therapy) who presents today for end-of-treatment assessment following completion of her sixth and final cycle of adjuvant gem plus cape. She returns for review of restaging imaging, tumor markers, and to establish a surveillance plan.

---

**HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS**

Ms. Osei-Bonsu was originally diagnosed approximately 17 months ago with a 3.8 cm borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the head and uncinate process, with tumor abutting the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) over more than 180 degrees of circumference and directly contacting the superior mesenteric vein (SMV). Her initial CA 19-9 was markedly elevated at 1,247 U/mL. She underwent EUS-guided biopsy confirming moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. Comprehensive genomic profiling identified a KRAS G12V driver mutation, TP53 R248W, SMAD4 homozygous deletion, and CDKN2A homozygous deletion. She was found to carry a germline ATM frameshift pathogenic variant on subsequent germline testing; a low-VAF BRCA2 splice variant detected on the tumor biopsy was not confirmed on germline testing and is considered somatic-only.

Given her good performance status (ECOG 1), intact organ function, and absence of distant metastatic disease, the multidisciplinary pancreatic cancer tumor board recommended neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX) with the intent of downsizing and potentially converting to resectability. She received 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX (Eloxatin dose-reduced to 68 mg/m2 after cycle 2 for grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy, bolus 5-FU omitted per modified regimen). Her CA 19-9 declined steadily — from 1,247 to 620 after cycle 2, to 310 after cycle 4, and to 85 U/mL after all 8 cycles. Restaging CT after 8 cycles showed the pancreatic mass had decreased from 3.8 x 3.2 cm to 2.6 x 2.1 cm, with the SMA contact reduced to approximately 90 degrees.

Given residual SMA abutment, the tumor board recommended consolidative conventionally fractionated chemoradiation therapy: IMRT to 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions with concurrent Xeloda (825 mg/m2 BID on radiation days). She tolerated this reasonably well with grade 2 nausea, grade 2 fatigue, grade 2 radiation-induced gastritis, and grade 1 hand-foot syndrome, all managed without treatment interruption. A 3 kg weight loss occurred during radiation; nutritional counseling and oral supplementation were initiated.

Restaging CT approximately two months after completing chemoradiation showed further reduction to 2.3 x 1.9 cm with SMA contact estimated at 60-70 degrees, and the SMV appearing patent with only minimal residual soft tissue thickening. CA 19-9 at that point was 72 U/mL. The tumor board confirmed resectability, and she proceeded to pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) approximately 9 months after initial diagnosis. Intraoperatively, the tumor was grossly adherent to but separable from the SMA; venous reconstruction of the SMV was not required. Final surgical pathology confirmed moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma measuring 2.1 x 1.8 cm, with extensive treatment effect (CAP tumor regression score 2). All surgical margins were negative — the posterior/SMA margin was the tightest at 0.1 cm but confirmed negative. Notably, all 22 sampled lymph nodes were free of malignancy (ypN0), yielding a final pathologic stage of ypT2N0M0.

Her post-operative course was complicated by a grade B pancreatic fistula that developed by post-operative day 7, manifesting as elevated drain amylase, low-grade fever, and drain output of approximately 180 mL/day. This was managed conservatively with bowel rest, total parenteral nutrition (TPN), and subcutaneous octreotide. The fistula gradually resolved over approximately five weeks without radiologic or operative intervention. She was discharged on post-operative day 18 and her drain was removed at her five-week post-operative visit. Her post-operative CA 19-9 nadir was 48 U/mL.

Adjuvant chemotherapy was initiated approximately 10 weeks post-surgery with Gemzar 1000 mg/m2 on days 1, 8, and 15 of a 28-day cycle plus cape per the ESPAC-4 regimen. During cycles 1 through 3, she developed grade 2 hand-foot syndrome necessitating a Xeloda dose reduction to 75% (1250 mg/m2/day), as well as grade 2 fatigue and grade 1 myelosuppression. Imaging at cycle 3 demonstrated resolving anastomotic fluid collection (down to 2.8 cm from 3.2 cm), no hepatic lesions, no peritoneal disease, and CA 19-9 of 32 U/mL. During cycles 5 and 6, she developed grade 3 fatigue requiring a one-week dose delay between cycles 5 and 6, and grade 2 thrombocytopenia (platelet nadir 72 x10^9/L) requiring Gemzar dose reduction to 75% for cycle 6. She completed cycle 6 with dose modifications; total adjuvant course completed.

Today she presents for her end-of-treatment visit. Restaging CT of chest, abdomen, and pelvis obtained approximately one month after completing cycle 6 is available for review. CA 19-9 and CEA are available.

---

**INTERVAL HISTORY AND REVIEW OF SYSTEMS**

Ms. Osei-Bonsu reports her energy level has begun to gradually improve over the past three to four weeks since completing her last chemotherapy cycle. She denies new or worsening abdominal pain. She continues to experience mild bilateral distal hand and foot paresthesias, which she attributes to residual Eloxatin-related neuropathy; these have slowly improved over the past several months and she rates them as mild (grade 1), no longer interfering with daily activities. She continues to use pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (pancrelipase) with all meals and snacks, which has markedly reduced her steatorrhea; she does report occasional loose stools when she forgets to take her enzymes with a meal. She is managing her post-pancreatectomy insulin-dependent diabetes with her endocrinologist; her most recent HbA1c was 8.2% — not at target but improving. She denies hypoglycemic episodes.

She reports mild, intermittent epigastric discomfort, which she relates to dietary indiscretion and for which she takes her prescribed proton pump inhibitor; no hematemesis, melena, or hematochezia. No nausea or vomiting currently. Appetite improving — she reports eating three small meals daily with supplementation. She has maintained her weight over the past month after a slow trajectory of recovery.

She denies any new back pain, right upper quadrant pain, or jaundice that might suggest hepatic or biliary involvement. No new lymphadenopathy appreciated by the patient. No cough, dyspnea, or hemoptysis. No bone pain. No new neurologic symptoms beyond the pre-existing neuropathy. No fever or night sweats. No lower extremity edema.

Psychosocially, she reports significant emotional relief at completing her treatment course. She notes ongoing anxiety about recurrence, which is understandable given the natural history of this disease and the intensity of her treatment. She has been referred to the cancer survivorship clinic and is interested in a peer support group for pancreatic cancer survivors. She lives alone but has strong family support from her adult children who accompanied her to most of her treatment appointments.

Medications: Metformin 500 mg BID (pre-existing diabetes; continued throughout), insulin glargine 18 units at bedtime with insulin lispro sliding scale at meals (post-pancreatectomy diabetes), lisinopril 10 mg daily (hypertension), amlodipine 5 mg daily (hypertension added during treatment), pantoprazole 40 mg daily, pancrelipase (Creon) 36,000 lipase units per capsule — two capsules with main meals, one with snacks, loperamide as needed for diarrhea (using infrequently now), pyridoxine 100 mg daily (for hand-foot syndrome prevention), multivitamin with fat-soluble vitamins (for malabsorption), and calcium/vitamin D supplementation.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

---

**PHYSICAL EXAMINATION**

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 128/76 mmHg, heart rate 74 bpm and regular, respiratory rate 14 breaths per minute, temperature 36.8°C (98.2°F), oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Weight 57.8 kg (pre-illness weight approximately 61 kg; she is approximately 3.2 kg below her pre-treatment baseline, which represents stabilization and modest improvement from her post-operative nadir). Height 162 cm. BMI 22.0.

General: Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a well-appearing, alert, and oriented 66-year-old woman in no acute distress. She is pleasantly conversant and appropriately inquisitive. Affect is mildly anxious but appropriate to the context.

Head, Eyes, Ears, Nose, Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae anicteric. Conjunctivae without pallor. Oropharynx clear; no mucositis (resolved from prior treatment). No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy appreciated.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Peripheral pulses 2+ and symmetric bilaterally.

Pulmonary: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze, crackle, or rhonchi.

Abdomen: Midline and right upper quadrant well-healed Whipple incision without erythema, dehiscence, or herniation at the incision site. Abdomen soft and non-tender to palpation. No palpable hepatomegaly or splenomegaly. No palpable abdominal mass. No ascites appreciated on percussion or exam. Bowel sounds normoactive.

Extremities: No lower extremity edema. No calf tenderness.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Mild diminished light touch sensation in bilateral distal feet and fingers, symmetric, consistent with residual oxali-induced peripheral neuropathy — significantly improved from prior grade 2. Gait steady. No ataxia.

Skin: Palms and soles with mild residual post-inflammatory changes from prior hand-foot syndrome; no active fissuring, blistering, or grade 2 changes. No significant alopecia at this visit.

Performance Status: ECOG 1.

---

**LABORATORY AND TUMOR MARKER RESULTS**

CA 19-9: 21 U/mL (reference range <37 U/mL) — within normal limits. This represents a remarkable decline from 1,247 U/mL at diagnosis. Trajectory: 1,247 → 620 (after 2 cycles mFOLFIRINOX) → 310 (after 4 cycles) → 85 (after 8 cycles) → 72 (post-chemoradiation) → 48 (post-Whipple nadir) → 32 (during adjuvant, cycle 3) → 21 U/mL (current — normal range).

CEA: 2.1 ng/mL (reference range <5.0 ng/mL) — normalized from 8.4 ng/mL at diagnosis.

Complete blood count: WBC 5.4 x10^9/L (ANC 3.2 x10^9/L), hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL (mild anemia, improved from nadir), platelets 168 x10^9/L (recovered from nadir of 72 x10^9/L). No cytopenias of concern.

Comprehensive metabolic panel: Sodium 138 mEq/L, potassium 4.1 mEq/L, creatinine 0.82 mg/dL (eGFR >60), total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, ALT 28 U/L, AST 31 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 72 U/L — all within normal limits. Albumin 3.6 g/dL (mildly low; stable to improving from post-operative period).

HbA1c (from recent endocrinology visit): 8.2% — suboptimal but improving; diabetes management ongoing with endocrinology.

---

**IMAGING REVIEW**

Restaging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast, obtained approximately one month after completing cycle 6 of adjuvant Gemzar/Xeloda:

The post-Whipple surgical anatomy is well-established and stable. The pancreaticojejunostomy appears intact. The previously noted anastomotic fluid collection adjacent to the pancreaticojejunostomy has completely resolved — no residual collection or loculated fluid is identified. The surgical bed in the region of the pancreatic head and uncinate process shows expected post-operative soft tissue changes and fibrosis, without any nodular enhancing lesion, soft tissue mass, or appearance concerning for local recurrence at the resection bed.

The liver is normal in size and homogeneous in attenuation throughout, with no hypodense, hypervascular, or otherwise suspicious lesions on arterial, portal venous, or delayed phases of imaging. No biliary ductal dilation. The intrahepatic biliary system is decompressed (previously moderately dilated at diagnosis). The surgical hepaticojejunostomy anastomosis appears patent.

No peritoneal thickening, nodularity, or ascites is identified to suggest peritoneal disease. The mesentery appears normal. No para-aortic, retroperitoneal, mesenteric, or pelvic lymph nodes enlarged beyond 1 cm in short axis. The celiac axis and its branches, the SMA, and the SMV are patent without encasement or thrombus. There is expected post-surgical soft tissue at the SMA margin consistent with fibrotic changes from prior surgery and radiation.

On the CT of the chest, there are no pulmonary nodules, masses, or consolidations. No pleural effusion. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy.

Overall impression: No radiographic evidence of local recurrence, hepatic metastases, peritoneal disease, nodal disease, or pulmonary metastases. Complete resolution of the post-operative anastomotic fluid collection. Findings consistent with no evidence of disease following completion of multimodality treatment.

---

**ASSESSMENT AND PLAN**

Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old woman with previously borderline resectable pancreatic head ductal adenocarcinoma who has completed a full multimodality curative-intent treatment program. She presents today for her end-of-treatment assessment and transition to surveillance.

**Summary of treatment course:**
- Neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX x 8 cycles (with oxali dose reduction at cycle 3 for peripheral neuropathy)
- Consolidative chemoradiation: IMRT 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions with concurrent cape
- Pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure): R0 resection, ypT2N0M0 (0/22 nodes positive), CAP tumor regression score 2
- Adjuvant gem plus Xeloda x 6 cycles (with cape dose reduction for HFS, Gemzar dose reduction in cycle 6 for thrombocytopenia)
- Post-operative grade B pancreatic fistula, now fully resolved

**Current status: No evidence of disease (NED).**

CA 19-9 has normalized to 21 U/mL. CEA has normalized to 2.1 ng/mL. Restaging CT demonstrates no radiographic evidence of recurrence or metastasis. This is an excellent response to the full treatment program.

**1. Disease surveillance:**
We discussed the importance of regular follow-up given the high risk of recurrence in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma even after R0 resection with pathologic nodal clearance. The current planned surveillance schedule is CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast and CA 19-9 (with CEA) every 3 months for the first two years post-Whipple, then every 6 months for years three through five, and then annually thereafter. Clinical oncology follow-up visits will occur every 3 months for the first two years. I reviewed with the patient that while current imaging and markers are favorable, the possibility of recurrence — particularly hepatic, peritoneal, or distant — remains substantial over the next two to three years, and that prompt reporting of any new symptoms is critical. The patient understands and expresses that she is highly motivated to maintain her follow-up schedule.

**2. Residual peripheral neuropathy:**
Ms. Osei-Bonsu has grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy, bilaterally in the hands and feet, from cumulative oxali exposure. This has been slowly improving since completion of oxali-containing therapy. No active treatment beyond supportive care is required at this time. We will monitor at each follow-up visit. She has been counseled about fall prevention and foot care given her concurrent diabetes, which compounds the neuropathy risk. Referral to physical therapy for proprioceptive exercises and gait assessment placed.

**3. Post-pancreatectomy pancreatic exocrine insufficiency:**
Patient is established on pancrelipase (Creon) replacement, which she is tolerating well. Occasional loose stools when enzymes are missed — reinforced compliance counseling. Continued co-management with gastroenterology and dietitian. Surveillance for fat-soluble vitamin deficiency (vitamins A, D, E, K) planned at next visit with serum levels; she is already on supplementation. Bone density screening to be considered given malabsorption risk and age.

**4. Post-pancreatectomy insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus:**
Co-managed with endocrinology. HbA1c 8.2% — suboptimal. Continued current insulin regimen. Emphasize importance of glycemic control for overall health, quality of life, and potentially for cancer recurrence risk reduction (evidence emerging, though not definitive). No adjustments to insulin regimen made at this visit; will defer to endocrinology.

**5. Germline ATM pathogenic variant:**
Ms. Osei-Bonsu carries a germline heterozygous ATM frameshift pathogenic variant. She has been fully counseled regarding implications for her own cancer risk monitoring (pancreatic cancer surveillance programs for ATM carriers, increased lifetime risk for other malignancies including breast cancer) and implications for first-degree relatives. She has informed her adult children and two of them are seeking genetic counseling. Breast cancer surveillance: she is already due for her annual mammogram; referral placed. Continued coordination with the genetics team.

**6. Hypertension:**
Blood pressure well controlled at 128/76 mmHg on lisinopril and amlodipine. Continue current regimen. Primary care management.

**7. Nutritional rehabilitation and weight recovery:**
Weight is currently approximately 3.2 kg below her pre-illness baseline. This likely reflects a combination of the Whipple procedure sequelae, adjuvant chemotherapy, and reduced caloric intake over the past year. She is encouraged to gradually increase caloric density with the assistance of the dietitian. Will recheck weight and albumin at the next visit to assess trajectory.

**8. Psychosocial support and survivorship:**
I spent considerable time today discussing her experience, her fears about recurrence, and her goals going forward. She was referred to the cancer survivorship program and a pancreatic cancer survivor peer support group. She expressed interest in the Pancreatic Cancer Action Network (PanCAN) patient resources. She will begin working with a survivorship nurse navigator. We discussed the importance of regular physical activity as tolerated for overall health, well-being, and potentially cancer recurrence risk. Light aerobic activity (walking) encouraged and progressed as tolerated.

**9. Return to care:**
Return to oncology clinic in 3 months for surveillance CT and tumor markers. Patient was given my direct contact information and instructed to call with any concerning new symptoms — particularly new abdominal pain, jaundice, significant unintentional weight loss, or new back pain — before the next scheduled visit.

Time spent with patient today: approximately 50 minutes, more than half of which was devoted to counseling and coordination of care.

_Electronically signed by Attending Physician, Gastrointestinal Medical Oncology_
